1:
"Then came the children of Israel, even the whole congregation, into the desert of Zin in the first month: and the people abode in Kadesh; and Miriam died there, and was buried there." The witnesses note the new generation come to Kadesh, and the death and burial of Miriam. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was thirty-eight years after the foregoing events, and not the congregation that came out of Egypt, whose carcasses had fallen in the wilderness as threatened. The witnesses note that Miriam, the prophetess and sister of Moses, died and was buried there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the whole congregation — a new generation, the old being dead; the thirty-eight years after — the long wandering nearly run; the Miriam died there — the prophetess taken; the the leaders pass — the servants mortal, God's work going on.

The witnesses agree the chapter opens at a solemn turning: nearly forty years have passed, the old generation is dead, and a new one stands at Kadesh; and Miriam, who had led the women in song at the Red sea, now dies — the first of the three who led Israel out to be taken, a reminder that even the great servants of God are mortal, and that the leaders pass while the LORD and his work go on.

So the witnesses make the verse Kadesh and Miriam: The witnesses note the the whole congregation: a new generation, the old being dead. The witnesses note the thirty-eight years after: the long wandering nearly run. The witnesses note the Miriam died there: the prophetess taken. The witnesses note the the leaders pass: the servants mortal, God's work going on. The expositors observe the the whole congregation: a new generation, the old being dead. The expositors observe the thirty-eight years after: the long wandering nearly run. The expositors observe the Miriam died there: the prophetess taken. The expositors observe the the leaders pass: the servants mortal, God's work going on. The believer is taught to mark the the whole congregation, the thirty-eight years after, the Miriam died there, the the leaders pass, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that the new generation came to Kadesh and Miriam died — the old generation gone, the leaders mortal, while the LORD and his work go on.
2:
"And there was no water for the congregation: and they gathered themselves together against Moses and against Aaron." The witnesses note the want of water, and the people gathering against Moses and Aaron. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was ordered for the trial of the new generation, to see whether they would behave better than their fathers in a like case. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the congregation in want of water in the arid desert, gathering against their leaders. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no water — the congregation in thirst; the a trial — to prove the new generation; the gathered against Moses — in anger, not in prayer; the the fathers' sin repeated — the children running in the old channels.

The witnesses agree the lack of water was a test, and the new generation failed it as their fathers had: rather than seek the LORD, they gathered in anger against Moses and Aaron, repeating the old sin of murmuring; the resemblance is sobering — that the children, who had seen their fathers perish for unbelief, fell into the very same rebellion the moment they were tried, proof that the corrupt heart, untouched by grace, runs in the old channels.

So the witnesses make the verse the want of water: The witnesses note the no water: the congregation in thirst. The witnesses note the a trial: to prove the new generation. The witnesses note the gathered against Moses: in anger, not in prayer. The witnesses note the the fathers' sin repeated: the children running in the old channels. The expositors observe the no water: the congregation in thirst. The expositors observe the a trial: to prove the new generation. The expositors observe the gathered against Moses: in anger, not in prayer. The expositors observe the the fathers' sin repeated: the children running in the old channels. The believer is taught to mark the no water, the a trial, the gathered against Moses, the the fathers' sin repeated, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that the want of water tried the new generation, and they gathered against Moses as their fathers had — the corrupt heart, untouched by grace, running in the old channels of murmuring.
3:
"And the people chode with Moses, and spake, saying, Would God that we had died when our brethren died before the LORD!" The witnesses note the people quarrelling with Moses, wishing they had died with their brethren. The witnesses note that Gill notes they contended in a wrangling, litigious manner, showing no reverence for his office or the favours received — and this at a time when they should rather have condoled him on the loss of Miriam. The witnesses note that they wished they had perished with the rebels who died before the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the chode with Moses — quarrelling in a litigious manner; the no reverence — forgetting his office and past favours; the in his grief — reproaching him in the hour of Miriam's death; the would we had died — the desperate wish of unbelief.

The witnesses agree the murmuring was both impious and unkind: they quarrelled with Moses in his very hour of grief for his sister, and uttered the same desperate wish their fathers had — that they had died under God's hand; the ingratitude that forgets all past mercy in a present want, and the cruelty that adds reproach to a leader's sorrow, mark the hardness of a people who would rather have perished than trust the God who had borne them forty years.

So the witnesses make the verse the chiding people: The witnesses note the chode with Moses: quarrelling in a litigious manner. The witnesses note the no reverence: forgetting his office and past favours. The witnesses note the in his grief: reproaching him in the hour of Miriam's death. The witnesses note the would we had died: the desperate wish of unbelief. The expositors observe the chode with Moses: quarrelling in a litigious manner. The expositors observe the no reverence: forgetting his office and past favours. The expositors observe the in his grief: reproaching him in the hour of Miriam's death. The expositors observe the would we had died: the desperate wish of unbelief. The believer is taught to mark the chode with Moses, the no reverence, the in his grief, the would we had died, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that the people chode with Moses in his grief, wishing they had died — ingratitude forgetting past mercy, cruelty adding reproach to a leader's sorrow.
4:
"And why have ye brought up the congregation of the LORD into this wilderness, that we and our cattle should die there?" The witnesses note the people charging Moses and Aaron with bringing them into the wilderness to die. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were now come, by various marches and stations, into the wilderness of Zin. The witnesses note that they accused their leaders of bringing "the congregation of the LORD" to perish there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the why brought us up — blaming Moses for the wilderness; the the congregation of the LORD — claiming the name while quarrelling; the to die there — dreading death for themselves and cattle; the blaming the instrument — forgetting it was God who led them.

The witnesses agree the complaint, like their fathers', laid their distress at the wrong door: they blamed Moses for leading them where God himself had led them, and called themselves "the congregation of the LORD" even while quarrelling against the LORD's own guidance; the unbelief that cannot see God's hand in a hard providence blames the instrument, forgetting that it was the cloud and not Moses that had brought them to this place, and that he who led them there could surely sustain them.

So the witnesses make the verse the misplaced charge: The witnesses note the why brought us up: blaming Moses for the wilderness. The witnesses note the the congregation of the LORD: claiming the name while quarrelling. The witnesses note the to die there: dreading death for themselves and cattle. The witnesses note the blaming the instrument: forgetting it was God who led them. The expositors observe the why brought us up: blaming Moses for the wilderness. The expositors observe the the congregation of the LORD: claiming the name while quarrelling. The expositors observe the to die there: dreading death for themselves and cattle. The expositors observe the blaming the instrument: forgetting it was God who led them. The believer is taught to mark the why brought us up, the the congregation of the LORD, the to die there, the blaming the instrument, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that they charged Moses with bringing them to die in the wilderness — unbelief blaming the instrument, forgetting that God himself had led them there and could sustain them.
5:
"And wherefore have ye made us to come up out of Egypt, to bring us in unto this evil place? it is no place of seed, or of figs, or of vines, or of pomegranates; neither is there any water to drink." The witnesses note the people, as their fathers, longing for Egypt and reviling the wilderness as an evil place. The witnesses note that Gill notes they represented the matter as if forced out of Egypt against their wills, forgetting the bondage and bitter slavery they had cried out under. The witnesses note that they reviled the place as barren — no seed, figs, vines, or pomegranates, nor water. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made us come up — calling deliverance an injury; the this evil place — reviling the wilderness; the no figs or vines — seeing only the present barrenness; the the heart in Egypt — despising the redemption past and the land to come.

The witnesses agree the murmur betrayed the old root of unbelief — a heart still hankering after Egypt: they spoke of their deliverance as if it were an injury, forgot the cruel bondage from which God had freed them, and counted the wilderness, the very road to the land of promise, an "evil place"; so the discontented eye sees only the present lack and never the goodness behind and the glory before, despising both the redemption past and the inheritance to come.

So the witnesses make the verse the longing for Egypt: The witnesses note the made us come up: calling deliverance an injury. The witnesses note the this evil place: reviling the wilderness. The witnesses note the no figs or vines: seeing only the present barrenness. The witnesses note the the heart in Egypt: despising the redemption past and the land to come. The expositors observe the made us come up: calling deliverance an injury. The expositors observe the this evil place: reviling the wilderness. The expositors observe the no figs or vines: seeing only the present barrenness. The expositors observe the the heart in Egypt: despising the redemption past and the land to come. The believer is taught to mark the made us come up, the this evil place, the no figs or vines, the the heart in Egypt, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that they longed for Egypt and reviled the wilderness as evil — the discontented eye seeing only the present lack, despising both the redemption behind and the inheritance before.
6:
"And Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and they fell upon their faces: and the glory of the LORD appeared unto them." The witnesses note Moses and Aaron withdrawing to the tabernacle, falling on their faces, and the glory of the LORD appearing. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went like fugitives, fleeing through fear lest the people stone them, to the door of the tabernacle where the Lord had promised to meet Moses. The witnesses note that Calvin judges they fled in fear, yet to seek the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from the assembly — withdrawing from the raging people; the fell on their faces — prayer their refuge in danger; the the tabernacle door — where God had promised to meet them; the the glory appeared — the pledge that God had not forsaken them.

The witnesses agree the leaders did again what they had always done — fled to God: hard pressed and in danger, they withdrew from the raging assembly to the tabernacle door, fell on their faces, and there the glory of the LORD appeared to relieve them; the instinct of the faithful in trouble is to carry the matter to God, and his glory shining forth is the pledge that he had not forsaken his servants nor his murmuring people.

So the witnesses make the verse fled to the tabernacle: The witnesses note the from the assembly: withdrawing from the raging people. The witnesses note the fell on their faces: prayer their refuge in danger. The witnesses note the the tabernacle door: where God had promised to meet them. The witnesses note the the glory appeared: the pledge that God had not forsaken them. The expositors observe the from the assembly: withdrawing from the raging people. The expositors observe the fell on their faces: prayer their refuge in danger. The expositors observe the the tabernacle door: where God had promised to meet them. The expositors observe the the glory appeared: the pledge that God had not forsaken them. The believer is taught to mark the from the assembly, the fell on their faces, the the tabernacle door, the the glory appeared, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that Moses and Aaron fled to the tabernacle and fell on their faces, the glory appearing — the faithful carrying their trouble to God, his glory the pledge he had not forsaken them.
7:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the LORD speaking from his glory to relieve the people's want of water. The witnesses note that Gill notes God spake out of the token of his glory, perhaps the cloud with an uncommon brightness. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the LORD now relieved the want of water, directing Moses what to do. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — from the token of his glory; the to relieve the want — providing for the people's need; the mercy for murmuring — grace meeting ingratitude with supply; the the patience of God — answering sin with kindness, not the stroke.

The witnesses agree God answered murmuring with mercy: though the people had quarrelled and reviled, the LORD did not consume them but provided for their need, speaking from his glory to tell Moses how water should be given; the grace that meets ingratitude with supply, giving drink to a people who deserved drought, magnifies the patience of God, who often answers his people's sin with kindness rather than the stroke they have earned.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious word: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: from the token of his glory. The witnesses note the to relieve the want: providing for the people's need. The witnesses note the mercy for murmuring: grace meeting ingratitude with supply. The witnesses note the the patience of God: answering sin with kindness, not the stroke. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: from the token of his glory. The expositors observe the to relieve the want: providing for the people's need. The expositors observe the mercy for murmuring: grace meeting ingratitude with supply. The expositors observe the the patience of God: answering sin with kindness, not the stroke. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the to relieve the want, the mercy for murmuring, the the patience of God, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that the LORD spake from his glory to relieve the want of water — grace meeting murmuring with mercy, the patience of God answering sin with kindness.
8:
"Take the rod, and gather thou the assembly together, thou, and Aaron thy brother, and speak ye unto the rock before their eyes; and it shall give forth his water..." The witnesses note God's command — take the rod, gather the people, and speak to the rock, that it give water. The witnesses note that Gill notes the rod of miracles, the rod of Moses with which he had done wonders in Egypt and at Horeb, where he had smitten the rock the first year. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the command — take the rod, gather the assembly, and speak to the rock before their eyes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the take the rod — the rod of his former wonders; the gather the assembly — the people to witness; the speak to the rock — the word, not the blow, commanded; the it shall give water — god's power shown at his bare word.

The witnesses agree the command was precise, and its very terms framed the test of Moses' obedience: he was to take the rod but to speak to the rock, not smite it as before at Horeb; the change marks that this was a new sign — the rock once smitten needed now only the word — and the LORD would have his power shown by a simple command, that the water flow at his bare word and his glory be sanctified before all the people.

So the witnesses make the verse the command given: The witnesses note the take the rod: the rod of his former wonders. The witnesses note the gather the assembly: the people to witness. The witnesses note the speak to the rock: the word, not the blow, commanded. The witnesses note the it shall give water: God's power shown at his bare word. The expositors observe the take the rod: the rod of his former wonders. The expositors observe the gather the assembly: the people to witness. The expositors observe the speak to the rock: the word, not the blow, commanded. The expositors observe the it shall give water: God's power shown at his bare word. The believer is taught to mark the take the rod, the gather the assembly, the speak to the rock, the it shall give water, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that God bade Moses take the rod yet speak to the rock — a new sign, the water to flow at God's bare word, his glory to be sanctified before the people.
9:
"And Moses took the rod from before the LORD, as he commanded him." The witnesses note Moses taking the rod from before the LORD, laid up in the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Gill notes the rod was laid up somewhere in the sanctuary, as was Aaron's rod. The witnesses note that Wesley reads "from before the LORD" as out of the tabernacle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took the rod — beginning in obedience; the from before the LORD — out of the sanctuary where it lay; the as commanded — obeying thus far exactly; the the more grievous fall — his long obedience no guard against one failure.

The witnesses agree this far Moses obeyed exactly: he took the rod from its place before the LORD, as commanded, beginning the work in obedience; the brief verse shows him setting out rightly, the rod that had wrought so many wonders in his hand — and makes the more grievous what follows, that a man so used to obey, and so far obedient here, should yet stumble in the act, his long faithfulness no guarantee against a single failure.

So the witnesses make the verse the rod taken: The witnesses note the took the rod: beginning in obedience. The witnesses note the from before the LORD: out of the sanctuary where it lay. The witnesses note the as commanded: obeying thus far exactly. The witnesses note the the more grievous fall: his long obedience no guard against one failure. The expositors observe the took the rod: beginning in obedience. The expositors observe the from before the LORD: out of the sanctuary where it lay. The expositors observe the as commanded: obeying thus far exactly. The expositors observe the the more grievous fall: his long obedience no guard against one failure. The believer is taught to mark the took the rod, the from before the LORD, the as commanded, the the more grievous fall, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that Moses took the rod in obedience, as commanded — beginning rightly, which makes the more grievous the failure to follow, his long faithfulness no guard against one fall.
10:
"And Moses and Aaron gathered the congregation together before the rock, and he said unto them, Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?" The witnesses note Moses gathering the people and breaking out in anger — "Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water?" The witnesses note that Gill notes they gathered not the elders only but as many of the congregation as could be assembled, before the rock to which they were directed. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Moses' word — "Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?" The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hear, ye rebels — spoken in passion; the must we fetch — seeming to take the glory to himself; the provoked past patience — the meek man unadvised in his lips (Ps. 106:33); the marring the moment — anger and self-assertion where God should be sanctified.

The witnesses agree the words betray the sin of Moses' heart: in calling the people "rebels" he spoke in passion, and in asking "must we fetch you water?" he seemed to take the glory to himself and Aaron, as if the wonder were theirs and not God's; the meekest of men, provoked past patience, here spoke unadvisedly with his lips (Ps. 106:33), his anger and self-assertion marring the very moment he should have sanctified the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the angry word: The witnesses note the hear, ye rebels: spoken in passion. The witnesses note the must we fetch: seeming to take the glory to himself. The witnesses note the provoked past patience: the meek man unadvised in his lips (Ps. 106:33). The witnesses note the marring the moment: anger and self-assertion where God should be sanctified. The expositors observe the hear, ye rebels: spoken in passion. The expositors observe the must we fetch: seeming to take the glory to himself. The expositors observe the provoked past patience: the meek man unadvised in his lips (Ps. 106:33). The expositors observe the marring the moment: anger and self-assertion where God should be sanctified. The believer is taught to mark the hear, ye rebels, the must we fetch, the provoked past patience, the marring the moment, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that Moses called the people rebels and said "must we fetch you water?" — speaking in passion and self-assertion, the meek man unadvised in his lips (Ps. 106:33), marring the moment he should have sanctified the LORD.
11:
"And Moses lifted up his hand, and with his rod he smote the rock twice: and the water came out abundantly, and the congregation drank, and their beasts also." The witnesses note Moses smiting the rock twice — instead of speaking to it — and the water flowing abundantly. The witnesses note that Gill notes at first it brought but a few drops, so he smote again, and it flowed plentifully; he relays the Targum's tradition of blood at the first stroke and water at the second. The witnesses note that the water came out abundantly, and the people and their cattle drank. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smote the rock twice — striking where he should have spoken; the water came abundantly — god's mercy overflowing the fault; the that Rock was Christ — the smitten rock the smitten Saviour (1 Cor. 10:4); the the type marred — christ smitten but once, not twice (John 19:34).

The witnesses agree God's mercy overflowed even Moses' fault: though Moses smote where he should have spoken, and smote twice, the LORD still gave water abundantly for the murmuring people; and the rock itself, the apostle teaches, was Christ — "that Rock was Christ" (1 Cor. 10:4) — the smitten rock figuring the smitten Saviour from whom the water of life flows; Gill notes how the tradition of blood and water answers the blood and water from Christ's pierced side (John 19:34), yet Moses' double smiting marred the type, for Christ was to be smitten but once.

So the witnesses make the verse the rock smitten: The witnesses note the smote the rock twice: striking where he should have spoken. The witnesses note the water came abundantly: God's mercy overflowing the fault. The witnesses note the that Rock was Christ: the smitten rock the smitten Saviour (1 Cor. 10:4). The witnesses note the the type marred: Christ smitten but once, not twice (John 19:34). The expositors observe the smote the rock twice: striking where he should have spoken. The expositors observe the water came abundantly: God's mercy overflowing the fault. The expositors observe the that Rock was Christ: the smitten rock the smitten Saviour (1 Cor. 10:4). The expositors observe the the type marred: Christ smitten but once, not twice (John 19:34). The believer is taught to mark the smote the rock twice, the water came abundantly, the that Rock was Christ, the the type marred, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that Moses smote the rock twice and water flowed abundantly — God's mercy overflowing the fault, the rock a type of Christ smitten (1 Cor. 10:4), yet the double stroke marring the figure of the Saviour smitten but once.
12:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron, Because ye believed me not, to sanctify me in the eyes of the children of Israel, therefore ye shall not bring this congregation into the land which I have given them." The witnesses note the sentence — because Moses and Aaron believed not, to sanctify God, they should not bring Israel into the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD spake out of the cloud where his glory still appeared. The witnesses note that Clarke analyses the offence: God bade Moses speak to the rock, but he smote it, and twice, and said "must we fetch water," arrogating to himself and Aaron what was God's, and in anger. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye believed me not — the root of the sin unbelief; the to sanctify me — failing to honour God before the people; the not bring them in — barred from the land he sought; the the more required — the great servant judged for one fall.

The witnesses agree the root of the sin was unbelief that failed to honour God: Moses did not trust the bare word would suffice, smote in anger, and took the glory to himself, and so failed "to sanctify" the LORD before the people; the sentence is solemn and instructive — that even so great and meek a servant, for one act of unbelief and self-assertion, was barred from the land he had toiled forty years to reach, a proof that God will be hallowed in them that come near him, and that the nearer the servant, the more is required.

So the witnesses make the verse the sentence on Moses: The witnesses note the ye believed me not: the root of the sin unbelief. The witnesses note the to sanctify me: failing to honour God before the people. The witnesses note the not bring them in: barred from the land he sought. The witnesses note the the more required: the great servant judged for one fall. The expositors observe the ye believed me not: the root of the sin unbelief. The expositors observe the to sanctify me: failing to honour God before the people. The expositors observe the not bring them in: barred from the land he sought. The expositors observe the the more required: the great servant judged for one fall. The believer is taught to mark the ye believed me not, the to sanctify me, the not bring them in, the the more required, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that Moses and Aaron were barred from the land for unbelief that failed to sanctify God — God hallowed in them that come near, the nearer the servant, the more required.
13:
"This is the water of Meribah; because the children of Israel strove with the LORD, and he was sanctified in them." The witnesses note the place named Meribah, "strife," where Israel strove with the LORD and he was sanctified. The witnesses note that Gill notes Meribah means "strife," and this place is sometimes called Meribah-Kadesh to distinguish it from the earlier strife at Horeb. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note the name from the children of Israel striving with the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Meribah — the name meaning "strife."; the strove with the LORD — their quarrel against God himself; the he was sanctified — his mercy and his holiness both shown; the a double monument — of the people's sin and God's being hallowed.

The witnesses agree the name preserved the lesson of the day: "strife" — for the people strove with the LORD himself, not Moses only; yet even in their striving God "was sanctified," for he showed both his mercy in giving water and his holiness in judging the sin of his servants; the place stands as a double monument, of the people's perversity and of the God who is hallowed whether by the obedience of the willing or the judgment of the failing.

So the witnesses make the verse the water of strife: The witnesses note the Meribah: the name meaning "strife." The witnesses note the strove with the LORD: their quarrel against God himself. The witnesses note the he was sanctified: his mercy and his holiness both shown. The witnesses note the a double monument: of the people's sin and God's being hallowed. The expositors observe the Meribah: the name meaning "strife." The expositors observe the strove with the LORD: their quarrel against God himself. The expositors observe the he was sanctified: his mercy and his holiness both shown. The expositors observe the a double monument: of the people's sin and God's being hallowed. The believer is taught to mark the Meribah, the strove with the LORD, the he was sanctified, the a double monument, and to sanctify the LORD in his heart. The verse thus records that the place was named Meribah, "strife," where Israel strove with the LORD and he was sanctified — a monument of the people's perversity and of the God hallowed whether by obedience or by judgment.
14:
"And Moses sent messengers from Kadesh unto the king of Edom, Thus saith thy brother Israel, Thou knowest all the travail that hath befallen us:" The witnesses note Moses sending a peaceable embassy to the king of Edom, appealing to their brotherhood. The witnesses note that Gill notes Edom was sometimes governed by kings, sometimes by dukes; the king here, thirty-nine years after the Exodus, Usher takes to be Hadar. The witnesses note that Moses styled Israel "thy brother," Edom being descended from Esau, Jacob's brother. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sent messengers — a peaceable embassy, not an army; the thy brother Israel — owning the tie of kinship; the the king of Edom — the children of Esau; the peace not force — seeking by entreaty, forbidden to vex Edom (Deut. 2:4-5).

The witnesses agree Moses sought peace and pressed the tie of kinship: rather than force a passage, he sent envoys with a courteous request, owning Edom as a brother nation, the children of Esau and Jacob; the appeal to brotherhood was both wise and just, Israel forbidden to vex the descendants of Esau (cf. Deut. 2:4-5), and so seeking by entreaty what they would not take by the sword.

So the witnesses make the verse the embassy to Edom: The witnesses note the sent messengers: a peaceable embassy, not an army. The witnesses note the thy brother Israel: owning the tie of kinship. The witnesses note the the king of Edom: the children of Esau. The witnesses note the peace not force: seeking by entreaty, forbidden to vex Edom (Deut. 2:4-5). The expositors observe the sent messengers: a peaceable embassy, not an army. The expositors observe the thy brother Israel: owning the tie of kinship. The expositors observe the the king of Edom: the children of Esau. The expositors observe the peace not force: seeking by entreaty, forbidden to vex Edom (Deut. 2:4-5). The believer is taught to mark the sent messengers, the thy brother Israel, the the king of Edom, the peace not force, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Moses sent a peaceable embassy to Edom, appealing to brotherhood — seeking passage by entreaty, not the sword, Israel forbidden to vex the children of Esau (Deut. 2:4-5).
15:
"How our fathers went down into Egypt, and we have dwelt in Egypt a long time; and the Egyptians vexed us, and our fathers:" The witnesses note the embassy recounting Israel's history — the going down to Egypt and the long affliction there. The witnesses note that Gill notes this means Jacob and his twelve sons with their children going down into Egypt. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the recital — they dwelt in Egypt long, and the Egyptians vexed them and their fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the our fathers went down — jacob and his sons into Egypt; the dwelt a long time — the long sojourn there; the the Egyptians vexed us — the affliction Israel suffered; the courteous appeal — grounding the request in shared history.

The witnesses agree the appeal rested on a shared and known story: Moses recalled to Edom the bondage in Egypt and the affliction Israel had suffered, hoping that the memory of a kinsman's hard usage and deliverance would move compassion; the recital is a model of courteous diplomacy, grounding the request not in threat but in the common history and the pity due to a brother who had passed through great trouble.

So the witnesses make the verse the recital of bondage: The witnesses note the our fathers went down: Jacob and his sons into Egypt. The witnesses note the dwelt a long time: the long sojourn there. The witnesses note the the Egyptians vexed us: the affliction Israel suffered. The witnesses note the courteous appeal: grounding the request in shared history. The expositors observe the our fathers went down: Jacob and his sons into Egypt. The expositors observe the dwelt a long time: the long sojourn there. The expositors observe the the Egyptians vexed us: the affliction Israel suffered. The expositors observe the courteous appeal: grounding the request in shared history. The believer is taught to mark the our fathers went down, the dwelt a long time, the the Egyptians vexed us, the courteous appeal, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that the embassy recounted the bondage in Egypt — a courteous appeal grounding the request in shared history and the pity due a brother in trouble.
16:
"And when we cried unto the LORD, he heard our voice, and sent an angel, and hath brought us forth out of Egypt: and, behold, we are in Kadesh, a city in the uttermost of thy border:" The witnesses note the embassy telling of God's deliverance — the cry heard, the angel sent, Israel brought out and now at Edom's border. The witnesses note that Gill notes they cried to the LORD by reason of their bondage and to be delivered from it. The witnesses note that Ellicott refers the "angel" to the Angel of the LORD who went before them (Exod. 14:19). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we cried — israel's cry in bondage; the he heard, sent an angel — god's answer and deliverance (Exod. 14:19); the brought us forth — out of Egypt by his hand; the at thy border — standing respectfully, asking leave.

The witnesses agree the testimony honoured God before the heathen king: Israel ascribed their deliverance not to their own strength but to the LORD who heard their cry and sent his angel to bring them out; the embassy bore witness, even to Edom, of the God who saves his people, and now stood respectfully at the border, asking leave rather than presuming — a confession of God's mercy joined to a humble request.

So the witnesses make the verse the deliverance told: The witnesses note the we cried: Israel's cry in bondage. The witnesses note the he heard, sent an angel: God's answer and deliverance (Exod. 14:19). The witnesses note the brought us forth: out of Egypt by his hand. The witnesses note the at thy border: standing respectfully, asking leave. The expositors observe the we cried: Israel's cry in bondage. The expositors observe the he heard, sent an angel: God's answer and deliverance (Exod. 14:19). The expositors observe the brought us forth: out of Egypt by his hand. The expositors observe the at thy border: standing respectfully, asking leave. The believer is taught to mark the we cried, the he heard, sent an angel, the brought us forth, the at thy border, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that the embassy told of God's deliverance, the cry heard and the angel sent — a confession of God's mercy before the heathen king, joined to a humble request for passage.
17:
"Let us pass, I pray thee, through thy country: we will not pass through the fields, or through the vineyards, neither will we drink of the water of the wells: we will go by the king's high way..." The witnesses note the modest request — passage by the king's highway only, harming no field or vineyard, taking no well-water. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the nearest and shortest way to Canaan from where they were. The witnesses note that Clarke notes this is the first mention of "the king's highway," a public road made by the king's authority at the state's expense. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let us pass — only to march straight through; the not the fields or vineyards — harming no crop or vine; the not the well-water — taking nothing of Edom's; the by the king's highway — the public road, leaving no ground for fear.

The witnesses agree the request was studiously inoffensive: Israel asked only to march straight through by the public road, promising to touch no crop, vine, or well, that Edom might have no fear of plunder or harm; the humility and fairness of the petition — a great host offering to pass without taking so much as a drink — left Edom no honest ground for refusal, and made his churlishness the more inexcusable.

So the witnesses make the verse the modest request: The witnesses note the let us pass: only to march straight through. The witnesses note the not the fields or vineyards: harming no crop or vine. The witnesses note the not the well-water: taking nothing of Edom's. The witnesses note the by the king's highway: the public road, leaving no ground for fear. The expositors observe the let us pass: only to march straight through. The expositors observe the not the fields or vineyards: harming no crop or vine. The expositors observe the not the well-water: taking nothing of Edom's. The expositors observe the by the king's highway: the public road, leaving no ground for fear. The believer is taught to mark the let us pass, the not the fields or vineyards, the not the well-water, the by the king's highway, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Israel asked only to pass by the highway, harming nothing — a studiously inoffensive request that left Edom no honest ground for refusal.
18:
"And Edom said unto him, Thou shalt not pass by me, lest I come out against thee with the sword." The witnesses note Edom's blunt refusal, threatening the sword if Israel attempted to pass. The witnesses note that Gill notes the king of Edom replied to Israel by the messengers. The witnesses note that Wesley reads it an act of policy, to secure themselves from so numerous a host, refusing passage through their country. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou shalt not pass — the blunt denial; the lest I come with the sword — meeting entreaty with threat; the a brother turned away — denying kin in their need; the the old enmity — edom's unbrotherliness arraigned by the prophets (Obad. 10).

The witnesses agree the refusal was both unkind and faithless: Edom met a brother's humble request with a threat of war, denying the passage that would have cost him nothing; whether from fear of so great a multitude or from the old enmity of Esau's line, he turned away his own kin in their need — an unbrotherliness for which the prophets long after arraigned Edom (cf. Obad. 10), the sin of refusing help to a brother remembered against him.

So the witnesses make the verse Edom's refusal: The witnesses note the thou shalt not pass: the blunt denial. The witnesses note the lest I come with the sword: meeting entreaty with threat. The witnesses note the a brother turned away: denying kin in their need. The witnesses note the the old enmity: Edom's unbrotherliness arraigned by the prophets (Obad. 10). The expositors observe the thou shalt not pass: the blunt denial. The expositors observe the lest I come with the sword: meeting entreaty with threat. The expositors observe the a brother turned away: denying kin in their need. The expositors observe the the old enmity: Edom's unbrotherliness arraigned by the prophets (Obad. 10). The believer is taught to mark the thou shalt not pass, the lest I come with the sword, the a brother turned away, the the old enmity, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Edom bluntly refused and threatened the sword — meeting a brother's entreaty with hostility, the unbrotherliness long after arraigned against him (Obad. 10).
19:
"And the children of Israel said unto him, We will go by the high way: and if I and my cattle drink of thy water, then I will pay for it: only... I will go through on my feet." The witnesses note Israel pressing the request again, even offering to pay for any water they drank. The witnesses note that Gill notes the messengers made answer to the king of Edom. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the renewed offer — to go by the highway, pay for any water, and only pass through on foot, doing nothing else. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we will go by the highway — keeping to the public road; the pay for the water — offering to buy a drink, not seize it; the on my feet — simply passing through, taking nothing; the patience under churlishness — restraint under God's command.

The witnesses agree Israel answered churlishness with patience: they pressed the request once more, lowering it further still — they would keep to the road, pay for whatever water they used, and simply pass through on foot, taking nothing; the meekness of so great a people, ready to buy a drink rather than seize it, shines against Edom's hardness, and shows the restraint of those who walk under God's command not to provoke their brother.

So the witnesses make the verse the patient renewal: The witnesses note the we will go by the highway: keeping to the public road. The witnesses note the pay for the water: offering to buy a drink, not seize it. The witnesses note the on my feet: simply passing through, taking nothing. The witnesses note the patience under churlishness: restraint under God's command. The expositors observe the we will go by the highway: keeping to the public road. The expositors observe the pay for the water: offering to buy a drink, not seize it. The expositors observe the on my feet: simply passing through, taking nothing. The expositors observe the patience under churlishness: restraint under God's command. The believer is taught to mark the we will go by the highway, the pay for the water, the on my feet, the patience under churlishness, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Israel pressed the request again, offering to pay for water — meekness answering churlishness, the restraint of those under God's command not to provoke their brother.
20:
"And he said, Thou shalt not go through. And Edom came out against him with much people, and with a strong hand." The witnesses note Edom's final, peremptory refusal, coming out with a strong force to bar the way. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was an absolute and peremptory denial. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Edom, to give emphasis to his refusal, came out against Israel with much people and a strong hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou shalt not go through — the peremptory denial; the came out against him — an army marched to the border; the with a strong hand — the refusal backed by force; the hostility measured — ready to fight a kinsman over a harmless passage.

The witnesses agree Edom not only refused but made a show of force: the flat "thou shalt not go through" was backed by an army marched out to the border, that Israel might see his refusal was final and defended; the readiness to fight a kinsman rather than grant a harmless passage measures the depth of Edom's hostility, and sets in relief Israel's obedience in not forcing the way, though they could have.

So the witnesses make the verse the armed refusal: The witnesses note the thou shalt not go through: the peremptory denial. The witnesses note the came out against him: an army marched to the border. The witnesses note the with a strong hand: the refusal backed by force. The witnesses note the hostility measured: ready to fight a kinsman over a harmless passage. The expositors observe the thou shalt not go through: the peremptory denial. The expositors observe the came out against him: an army marched to the border. The expositors observe the with a strong hand: the refusal backed by force. The expositors observe the hostility measured: ready to fight a kinsman over a harmless passage. The believer is taught to mark the thou shalt not go through, the came out against him, the with a strong hand, the hostility measured, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Edom peremptorily refused and came out with a strong force — his readiness to fight a kinsman over a harmless passage measuring the depth of his hostility.
21:
"Thus Edom refused to give Israel passage through his border: wherefore Israel turned away from him." The witnesses note Edom's refusal final, and Israel turning away to take the longer road. The witnesses note that Gill notes the refusal stood notwithstanding their near relation and Israel's fair promises. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that Israel turned away from him and journeyed on. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Edom refused — the denial final, despite their kinship; the Israel turned away — declining to force the passage; the the longer road — the weary detour around Edom; the obedient yielding — suffering wrong rather than break God's command.

The witnesses agree Israel's turning away was an act of obedience, not weakness: though wronged by a kinsman and strong enough to force the way, they declined to fight Edom, because God had forbidden it, and took instead the long and weary detour around his land; the submission shows that the people of God are sometimes to yield their right and suffer wrong rather than break the command of God, leaving their cause and their road in his hands.

So the witnesses make the verse Israel turns away: The witnesses note the Edom refused: the denial final, despite their kinship. The witnesses note the Israel turned away: declining to force the passage. The witnesses note the the longer road: the weary detour around Edom. The witnesses note the obedient yielding: suffering wrong rather than break God's command. The expositors observe the Edom refused: the denial final, despite their kinship. The expositors observe the Israel turned away: declining to force the passage. The expositors observe the the longer road: the weary detour around Edom. The expositors observe the obedient yielding: suffering wrong rather than break God's command. The believer is taught to mark the Edom refused, the Israel turned away, the the longer road, the obedient yielding, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Edom refused and Israel turned away — an obedient yielding, suffering wrong rather than fight a kinsman God had forbidden them to vex, leaving their cause in his hands.
22:
"And the children of Israel, even the whole congregation, journeyed from Kadesh, and came unto mount Hor." The witnesses note the whole congregation journeying from Kadesh to Mount Hor, where Aaron would die. The witnesses note that Gill notes the "whole congregation" went from Kadesh complete, not one lost by Edom's coming out against them. The witnesses note that Henry observes the chapter began with the funeral of Miriam and ends with that of Aaron. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the whole congregation — journeying complete from Kadesh; the came unto mount Hor — the place of Aaron's death; the Miriam and Aaron — the chapter framed by two funerals; the the guides fall — the leaders taken while the people go on.

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the chapter's mournful frame: it opened with Miriam's grave and closes at Aaron's, the two elder of the three who led Israel out now taken; the journey to Mount Hor brings the people to the place of another funeral, a reminder that the pilgrimage of God's people is marked at its stages with the graves of their guides, who fall by the way while the congregation goes on toward the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse to Mount Hor: The witnesses note the the whole congregation: journeying complete from Kadesh. The witnesses note the came unto mount Hor: the place of Aaron's death. The witnesses note the Miriam and Aaron: the chapter framed by two funerals. The witnesses note the the guides fall: the leaders taken while the people go on. The expositors observe the the whole congregation: journeying complete from Kadesh. The expositors observe the came unto mount Hor: the place of Aaron's death. The expositors observe the Miriam and Aaron: the chapter framed by two funerals. The expositors observe the the guides fall: the leaders taken while the people go on. The believer is taught to mark the the whole congregation, the came unto mount Hor, the Miriam and Aaron, the the guides fall, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that the congregation journeyed to Mount Hor — the chapter framed by the graves of Miriam and Aaron, the guides falling by the way while the people go on toward the rest.
23:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron in mount Hor, by the coast of the land of Edom, saying," The witnesses note the LORD speaking at Mount Hor to declare the time of Aaron's death. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were at the foot of the mountain, in the valley adjoining. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the word at Mount Hor, by the coast of Edom, announcing Aaron's death. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — announcing Aaron's death beforehand; the in mount Hor — by the border of Edom; the with order — the priesthood to pass without breach; the God's care — over the death of his servants and his ordinances.

The witnesses agree God gave Aaron warning and order in his death: the LORD spoke beforehand to Moses and Aaron, that the high priest's end might be met with preparation and the priesthood handed on without breach; the dignity of the scene — God himself appointing the place, the manner, and the successor — shows his care over the death of his servants and the continuance of his ordinances, nothing left to chance even in the passing of so great a man.

So the witnesses make the verse the word at Mount Hor: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: announcing Aaron's death beforehand. The witnesses note the in mount Hor: by the border of Edom. The witnesses note the with order: the priesthood to pass without breach. The witnesses note the God's care: over the death of his servants and his ordinances. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: announcing Aaron's death beforehand. The expositors observe the in mount Hor: by the border of Edom. The expositors observe the with order: the priesthood to pass without breach. The expositors observe the God's care: over the death of his servants and his ordinances. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the in mount Hor, the with order, the God's care, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that the LORD spoke at Mount Hor to declare Aaron's death — God ordering the passing of his servant and the handing-on of the priesthood, nothing left to chance.
24:
"Aaron shall be gathered unto his people: for he shall not enter into the land which I have given unto the children of Israel, because ye rebelled against my word at the water of Meribah." The witnesses note Aaron's sentence — to be gathered to his people, barred from the land, because of the rebellion at Meribah. The witnesses note that Gill notes "gathered unto his people" is a periphrasis for death, used of good and bad alike. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the phrase does not refer to the place of burial (Gen. 25:8), and Wesley that the rebellion was one reason, though not the only one, God not having Aaron enter. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the gathered to his people — the gentle word for death; the not enter the land — barred for the sin at Meribah; the shared exclusion — dying outside, with Moses; the received home — death a going to the faithful gone before (Gen. 25:8).

The witnesses agree the sentence on Aaron, like that on Moses, was tempered with mercy yet firm in justice: he must die outside the land for the sin at Meribah, sharing his brother's exclusion; yet "gathered unto his people" speaks comfort, for death to the believer is a going home to the company of the faithful gone before — the high priest barred from the earthly Canaan but received to the better country, the temporal loss not the loss of his soul.

So the witnesses make the verse Aaron gathered: The witnesses note the gathered to his people: the gentle word for death. The witnesses note the not enter the land: barred for the sin at Meribah. The witnesses note the shared exclusion: dying outside, with Moses. The witnesses note the received home: death a going to the faithful gone before (Gen. 25:8). The expositors observe the gathered to his people: the gentle word for death. The expositors observe the not enter the land: barred for the sin at Meribah. The expositors observe the shared exclusion: dying outside, with Moses. The expositors observe the received home: death a going to the faithful gone before (Gen. 25:8). The believer is taught to mark the gathered to his people, the not enter the land, the shared exclusion, the received home, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Aaron was to be gathered to his people, barred from the land for Meribah — justice and mercy joined, the high priest received home though shut out of the earthly Canaan (Gen. 25:8).
25:
"Take Aaron and Eleazar his son, and bring them up unto mount Hor:" The witnesses note God bidding Moses bring Aaron and Eleazar his son up the mountain — the successor designated while the father yet lived. The witnesses note that Gill notes Eleazar was Aaron's eldest son, who was to succeed him in the priesthood, and did. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the successor was designated while Aaron yet lived, that the perpetuity of the priesthood be secured and the people not take the election upon themselves. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron and Eleazar — father and son up the mount; the the eldest son — to succeed in the priesthood; the designated while living — the succession without breach; the God's provision — the people never left without a priest.

The reformed reader (Calvin) draws out the wisdom of the order: Eleazar was installed before his father died, that the priesthood might pass without interruption and the people not presume, with their usual rashness, to choose a priest themselves, the office depending wholly on the will of God; the careful succession shows that God provides for the continuance of his ministry, the dying priest replaced before he falls, that the people be never left without one to draw near for them.

So the witnesses make the verse the successor brought up: The witnesses note the Aaron and Eleazar: father and son up the mount. The witnesses note the the eldest son: to succeed in the priesthood. The witnesses note the designated while living: the succession without breach. The witnesses note the God's provision: the people never left without a priest. The expositors observe the Aaron and Eleazar: father and son up the mount. The expositors observe the the eldest son: to succeed in the priesthood. The expositors observe the designated while living: the succession without breach. The expositors observe the God's provision: the people never left without a priest. The believer is taught to mark the Aaron and Eleazar, the the eldest son, the designated while living, the God's provision, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that God bade Moses bring Aaron and Eleazar up the mount — the successor designated while the father lived, God providing for the unbroken continuance of his ministry.
26:
"And strip Aaron of his garments, and put them upon Eleazar his son: and Aaron shall be gathered unto his people, and shall die there." The witnesses note the command to strip Aaron of his priestly garments and clothe Eleazar — the transfer of the office before Aaron died. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priestly garments, as the Targum says. The witnesses note that Clarke explains the stripping was in effect depriving Aaron of his office, and the clothing of Eleazar a transfer of it — an investiture, as removing one was a divestiture. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the strip Aaron — the ending of his office; the put them upon Eleazar — the conferring of the priesthood; the the robes passing — the office moving from father to son; the the mortal priesthood — its holders dying and replaced (Heb. 7:23).

The witnesses agree the changing of the garments was the visible passing of the priesthood: to strip Aaron was to end his office, to clothe Eleazar was to confer it, the high-priestly robes moving from father to son before the eyes of God; the rite figures both the mortality of the Aaronic priesthood — its holders dying and being replaced, one after another (Heb. 7:23) — and, by contrast, the unchanging priesthood of Christ, who, because he continues ever, has an office that passes to no successor.

So the witnesses make the verse the garments transferred: The witnesses note the strip Aaron: the ending of his office. The witnesses note the put them upon Eleazar: the conferring of the priesthood. The witnesses note the the robes passing: the office moving from father to son. The witnesses note the the mortal priesthood: its holders dying and replaced (Heb. 7:23). The expositors observe the strip Aaron: the ending of his office. The expositors observe the put them upon Eleazar: the conferring of the priesthood. The expositors observe the the robes passing: the office moving from father to son. The expositors observe the the mortal priesthood: its holders dying and replaced (Heb. 7:23). The believer is taught to mark the strip Aaron, the put them upon Eleazar, the the robes passing, the the mortal priesthood, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Aaron was stripped and Eleazar clothed — the visible passing of the priesthood, figuring the mortal Aaronic office handed on, set against the unchanging priesthood of Christ (Heb. 7:23).
27:
"And Moses did as the LORD commanded: and they went up into mount Hor in the sight of all the congregation." The witnesses note Moses obeying the hard command, the three going up the mount in the sight of all Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes how cutting and afflicting it must have been to Moses to part with a brother so dear, yet being the Lord's will he submitted and faithfully obeyed. The witnesses note that they went up in the sight of all the congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the did as commanded — submitting to the hard duty; the a brother so dear — the grief of parting; the went up the mount — the three ascending together; the in the sight of all — the act public and solemn.

The witnesses agree Moses' obedience in so painful a duty was a triumph of submission: to lead his own brother up the mountain to die, and with his own hands to take from him the priestly robes, was a grief few could bear, yet Moses did as the LORD commanded; and the going up "in the sight of all the congregation" made the act public and solemn, the people witnessing both the death of their high priest and the unbroken passing of his office.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses obeys: The witnesses note the did as commanded: submitting to the hard duty. The witnesses note the a brother so dear: the grief of parting. The witnesses note the went up the mount: the three ascending together. The witnesses note the in the sight of all: the act public and solemn. The expositors observe the did as commanded: submitting to the hard duty. The expositors observe the a brother so dear: the grief of parting. The expositors observe the went up the mount: the three ascending together. The expositors observe the in the sight of all: the act public and solemn. The believer is taught to mark the did as commanded, the a brother so dear, the went up the mount, the in the sight of all, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Moses obeyed the hard command, leading Aaron up to die in the sight of all Israel — a triumph of submission, the people witnessing the death of their high priest and the passing of his office.
28:
"And Moses stripped Aaron of his garments, and put them upon Eleazar his son; and Aaron died there in the top of the mount: and Moses and Eleazar came down from the mount." The witnesses note the command carried out — Aaron stripped, Eleazar clothed, Aaron dying on the top, and the two coming down. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same hands that clothed Aaron at first now stripped him, both at God's command; the stripping a figure of the disannulling of his priesthood when the Messiah, a priest after another order, should come. The witnesses note that Aaron died on the top of the mount and Moses and Eleazar came down. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stripped and clothed — the office passed to Eleazar; the died on the top — the high priest taken on the mount; the the same hands — that clothed him now stripping him; the the priesthood disannulled — superseded by Christ who dies no more (Heb. 7:24).

The witnesses agree the scene preaches the supersession of the Aaronic order: Aaron, stripped of his robes, dies on the mountain, and his office, passed to Eleazar, will pass again and again through dying men, until it is "disannulled" by the coming of Christ, a priest after the order of Melchizedek; the high priest going up clothed and coming down dead, his garments left on another, figures the weakness of a priesthood that death cut short, and the need of the one Priest who dies no more (Heb. 7:24).

So the witnesses make the verse Aaron dies: The witnesses note the stripped and clothed: the office passed to Eleazar. The witnesses note the died on the top: the high priest taken on the mount. The witnesses note the the same hands: that clothed him now stripping him. The witnesses note the the priesthood disannulled: superseded by Christ who dies no more (Heb. 7:24). The expositors observe the stripped and clothed: the office passed to Eleazar. The expositors observe the died on the top: the high priest taken on the mount. The expositors observe the the same hands: that clothed him now stripping him. The expositors observe the the priesthood disannulled: superseded by Christ who dies no more (Heb. 7:24). The believer is taught to mark the stripped and clothed, the died on the top, the the same hands, the the priesthood disannulled, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that Aaron was stripped, Eleazar clothed, and Aaron died on the mount — the supersession of a priesthood that death cut short, figuring the need of the one Priest who dies no more (Heb. 7:24).
29:
"And when all the congregation saw that Aaron was dead, they mourned for Aaron thirty days, even all the house of Israel." The witnesses note all Israel mourning for Aaron thirty days when they perceived he was dead. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did not see his body, but perceived it by Moses' report and the circumstances — Eleazar coming down with Aaron's garments on, and the tokens of mourning; the Targums add they saw Moses and Eleazar come down with garments rent and ashes on their heads. The witnesses note that the whole house of Israel mourned thirty days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the saw he was dead — perceived by the tokens, not the body; the mourned thirty days — a long and solemn grief; the all the house of Israel — the whole people weeping; the a public sorrow — the death of a faithful minister felt by all.

The witnesses agree the long and general mourning honoured the man and his office: all Israel, even the people who had so often murmured against him, wept for Aaron thirty days, the same term they would later keep for Moses; the universal grief shows that the high priest, for all the people's past unkindness, was loved and his loss felt — and that the death of a faithful minister of God is a public sorrow, the whole congregation the poorer for it.

So the witnesses make the verse the mourning for Aaron: The witnesses note the saw he was dead: perceived by the tokens, not the body. The witnesses note the mourned thirty days: a long and solemn grief. The witnesses note the all the house of Israel: the whole people weeping. The witnesses note the a public sorrow: the death of a faithful minister felt by all. The expositors observe the saw he was dead: perceived by the tokens, not the body. The expositors observe the mourned thirty days: a long and solemn grief. The expositors observe the all the house of Israel: the whole people weeping. The expositors observe the a public sorrow: the death of a faithful minister felt by all. The believer is taught to mark the saw he was dead, the mourned thirty days, the all the house of Israel, the a public sorrow, and to trust the LORD who leads his people. The verse thus records that all Israel mourned for Aaron thirty days — the long and general grief honouring the high priest, the death of a faithful minister a public sorrow felt by the whole congregation.
